g.text: “We will practice the proper path of an ascetic, so that our recognition will be true and factual”
ye dhammā samaṇakaraṇā ca brāhmaṇakaraṇā ca te dhamme samādāya vattissāma
“Do not let the goal pass you by, while there is still more to be done”
mā vo attho parihāyi, sati uttariṁ karaṇīye
Possessing an internal sense of right and wrong
alongside a steadfast fear of wrongdoing
Maintaining pure, open, and restrained behavior
that brings no reproach to oneself or others
that is completely free from blame and self-reproach
Not grasping at features, not enticed by details
Not for pleasure, not for indulgence — only for support of the body
Purifying the mind from obstructive states day and night
throughout every physical movement, posture, and daily activity
ABANDONING OF THE FIVE HINDRANCES · pañcannaṁ nīvaraṇānaṁ pahānaṁ
Simile · A person taking a loan to invest
on business success, and still have some profit left over
Simile · A person who is sick, in pain, and seriously ill
Gives up Ill will byāpāda pahāya
and their food nourishes them, and they regain strength
Simile · A person who is imprisoned
Gives up Dullness and Drowsiness thīnamiddha pahāya
safely and securely, without any loss to their belongings
Simile · A person who is a slave, not free, dependent on others
Gives up Restlessness and Worry uddhaccakukkucca pahāya
becoming independent, and free to go where they want
Like a dangerous path · yathā kantāraddhānamaggaṁ
Simile · A person with wealth traveling through a dangerous path
Like reaching a place of safety
securely, and without any loss to their belongings
“He sees these five hindrances as debt, disease, prison, slavery, a dangerous path.”
yathā iṇaṁ · yathā rogaṁ · yathā bandhanāgāraṁ · yathā dāsabyaṁ · yathā kantāraddhānamaggaṁ
When abandoned: free from debt, healthy, released, free, in a place of safety
Born of seclusion (vivekaja) · Imbued with uplifting joy and pleasure (pītisukha)
Free from reflection and examination (avitakka · avicāra)
Born of collectedness (samādhija) · Imbued with uplifting joy and pleasure (pītisukha)
With the fading of desire for uplifting joy (pītiyā ca virāgā)
Experiencing pleasure with the body (sukha kāyena)
Abandoning bodily pleasure and pain (sukhassa ca pahānā dukkhassa ca pahānā)
Settling of mental pleasure and displeasure (somanassadomanassānaṁ atthaṅgamā)
With mind collected, purified, clarified, blemish-free, flexible, stable, and unshakeable
samāhite citte parisuddhe pariyodāte anaṅgaṇe vigatūpakkilese mudubhūte kammaniye ṭhite āneñjappatte
Simile · traveler recollecting villages passed by
Sees beings passing away and reappearing
Simile · seeing comings and goings between two houses
its ending, and the way of practice
Simile · clear undisturbed mountain lake — contents visible
Mind is liberated from the taint of sensual desire, the taint of becoming, and the taint of ignorance.
“Birth is ended, the spiritual life has been lived, what was to be done has been done.”
khīṇā jāti, vusitaṁ brahmacariyaṁ, kataṁ karaṇīyaṁ, nāparaṁ itthattāyā
When harmful, unwholesome states are abandoned, one is called:
